perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, documents, graphics, electronic files, notes, and other written, printed, or electronic copies, or other tangible materials or digital files in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, documents, graphics, electronic files, notes, and other written, printed, or electronic copies, or other tangible materials or digital files in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information within 90 days of Disclosing Party requesting it in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return timeframe to 90 days contradicts California law which requires immediate return of confidential information upon request, especially after termination of a business relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.2 implies that misappropriation of trade secrets (which confidential information can be considered) can be enjoined. Delaying the return of confidential information for 90 days after a written request allows for potential misappropriation and contradicts the immediacy expected under trade secret law. While the CUTSA doesn't explicitly define 'immediate,' a 90-day delay is commercially unreasonable and increases the risk of misuse, thus conflicting with the intent to prevent misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay for returning confidential information, which contradicts the implied immediacy required for trade secret protection. California Civil Code Section 3426.2(a) states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined,' indicating that the law aims to prevent misappropriation. By allowing a 90-day delay, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect for a period of 6 months after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality period to 6 months may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, which allows for protection as long as the information remains a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426 defines trade secrets and their protection. Limiting the protection to 6 months, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret, contradicts the law's intent to protect trade secrets for as long as they maintain their secret and provide a competitive advantage. The modified term is too short and does not correlate to the actual lifespan of the trade secret, potentially allowing for legal misappropriation after the arbitrary 6-month period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the non-disclosure period to 6 months, while the original text extends it as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the intent of California Civil Code Section 3426, which aims to protect trade secrets for as long as they maintain their secret and provide a competitive advantage. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4, confirms that the title covers the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By limiting the protection to 6 months, the changed text potentially allows misappropriation after that period, conflicting with the intent to protect trade secrets as outlined in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: All matters affecting the interpretation of this Agreement and the rights of the parties hereto shall be governed by the laws of the United States and the State of California, without reference to rules governing choice of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: All matters affecting the interpretation of this Agreement and the rights of the parties hereto shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nevada, without reference to rules governing choice of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada could violate California's interest in protecting its businesses and employees, especially if the agreement is primarily performed in California, potentially leading to unenforceability under California law.
perturbation.law_citation: Application Group, Inc. v. Theding, 138 Cal. App. 4th 171 (2006)
perturbation.law_explanation: Theding establishes that a choice-of-law provision will not be enforced if it violates a strong public policy of California. If the NDA is primarily related to business operations within California and the confidential information is related to California-based activities, then applying Nevada law could undermine California's interest in protecting its trade secrets and the employment relationships of California residents. This change introduces a conflict with California’s public policy favoring the protection of its businesses and workforce.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces California law with Nevada law, which, according to the Perturbation and Law explanations, could undermine California's public policy of protecting its businesses and workforce. This introduces a conflict, as applying Nevada law might compromise the protection of trade secrets and employment relationships within California, especially if the agreement is primarily performed there.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party may share Confidential Information with employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties without requiring them to sign any non-disclosure agreements. Receiving Party will make a commercially reasonable effort to protect the information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for downstream NDAs for employees/contractors/agents who gain access to confidential information is a violation of the reasonable steps standard under California's trade secret law, and weakens the protection of the information, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Reasonable Steps Requirement
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, trade secret status is contingent on the owner taking reasonable steps to maintain secrecy. Failing to require downstream NDAs from those with access to the information weakens the protection and argues against the owner taking reasonable steps to maintain the secret. Replacing a clear obligation to secure NDAs with a vague "commercially reasonable effort" to protect the information falls short of the standard required to maintain trade secret status under California law, potentially causing it to lose legal protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California trade secret law. The original contract mandated downstream NDAs to protect confidential information. The changed text eliminates this requirement, only requiring a "commercially reasonable effort" to protect the information. The Law Explanation states that under CUTSA, maintaining trade secret status requires taking 'reasonable steps to maintain secrecy' and failing to require downstream NDAs weakens this protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CIV 3426.1) defines 'improper means' which includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By removing the requirement for downstream NDAs, the changed text makes it easier for a breach of the duty to maintain secrecy to occur without recourse, thus contradicting the reasonable steps requirement and weakening trade secret protection under California law.
